title: Advanced Systems Analysis
description: Analyze messy evidence, competing explanations, and system-level learning outcomes.

questions.prompt: Two sections use different quiz packs. Section A scores higher, but it also had more prior experience. What is the best interpretation?
choices: Prior experience is a plausible confounder, so the pack difference is not isolated
The pack difference is proven as the only cause
Prior experience cannot affect quiz scores
The lower-scoring section must have ignored instructions
questions.hint: Look for a factor that differs between sections and could affect scores.
questions.explanation: Prior experience may influence scores independently of the pack, so the causal interpretation is limited. For further study, review confounding and quasi-experimental comparisons.

questions.prompt: A dashboard shows average score improved, but beginners improved while advanced learners declined. What issue does the average hide?
choices: Different effects across learner subgroups
A missing quiz title
The impossibility of using averages
Proof that all learners improved
questions.hint: A single average can hide subgroup patterns.
questions.explanation: Aggregate metrics can mask heterogeneous effects across learner groups. For further study, review subgroup analysis and Simpson's paradox.

questions.prompt: Selection bias can occur when the learners who choose a quiz differ systematically from those who do not.
questions.hint: Voluntary participation can change who is represented.
questions.explanation: If participants differ from nonparticipants, outcomes may reflect those differences as well as the quiz. For further study, review selection bias and sampling.

questions.prompt: When a measure captures something different from the intended construct, the interpretation has weak _____.
questions.hint: This word concerns whether the interpretation fits the evidence.
questions.explanation: Validity concerns the degree to which evidence supports the intended interpretation and use of scores. For further study, review validity arguments.

questions.prompt: Completion rate rises after hints are added, but average score falls. Which next question is most useful?
choices: Did hints bring in more learners who attempted harder material or had lower starting knowledge?
Can completion and score never move in different directions?
Should all hints be removed without review?
Did the app stop counting completions?
questions.hint: A participation change can alter who is included in the score average.
questions.explanation: Higher completion may include more or different learners, changing the average score population. For further study, review composition effects and metric interpretation.

questions.prompt: A new pack is tested only with learners who already enjoy the topic. What external-validity concern appears?
choices: Results may not generalize to less interested learners
The pack cannot be enjoyable for anyone
Interest makes all measures invalid
No learner characteristic can affect outcomes
questions.explanation: Testing only with interested learners limits generalization to broader audiences. For further study, review external validity and sample representativeness.

questions.prompt: A null result always means the tested strategy has no effect in any context.
questions.hint: A non-difference can have several explanations.
questions.explanation: A null result may reflect no effect, low power, poor implementation, insensitive measures, or context limits. For further study, review statistical power and implementation fidelity.

questions.prompt: Which evidence best supports a claim that a quiz improves transfer?
choices: Learners perform better on new scenario types that require the same underlying principle
Learners remember the exact wording of one original question
Learners click through the quiz quickly
Learners prefer the quiz title
questions.hint: Transfer requires success beyond the original surface form.
questions.explanation: Improvement on new scenarios with the same principle is direct evidence of transfer. For further study, review near and far transfer measures.

questions.prompt: A metric tracks time spent but not whether learners are engaged with the task. What measurement risk is present?
choices: Time may be an imperfect proxy for meaningful engagement
Time always proves deep engagement
Engagement cannot be measured in any way
The metric is perfect because it is numeric
questions.hint: A proxy is not the same as the construct.
questions.explanation: Time spent can reflect engagement, confusion, distraction, or idle behavior, so it is an imperfect proxy. For further study, review proxy measures and construct validity.

questions.prompt: The degree to which a result can apply beyond the original setting is external _____.
questions.explanation: External validity concerns whether findings generalize to other learners, settings, materials, or times. For further study, review generalizability and ecological validity.

questions.prompt: A system improves average score by making the easiest packs more visible. What outcome should analysts check next?
choices: Whether learning improved or learners shifted toward easier content
Whether the average score is now the only metric needed
Whether hard packs should be hidden permanently
Whether titles became shorter
questions.hint: Metric movement can come from behavior changes unrelated to learning.
questions.explanation: Average score can rise if learners choose easier packs, even without deeper learning. For further study, review metric gaming and construct-aligned outcomes.

questions.prompt: A study compares learners before and after a redesign, but another course requirement changed at the same time. What should be documented?
choices: The concurrent change as a possible alternative explanation
Only the redesign, because other changes never matter
Nothing, because documentation weakens claims
The favorite color of each learner
questions.hint: Changes that happen together complicate causal claims.
questions.explanation: Concurrent changes can explain outcomes, so they should be documented as alternative explanations. For further study, review threats to internal validity.

questions.prompt: A metric can be useful for monitoring while still being insufficient for a causal conclusion.
questions.hint: Monitoring and causal inference require different strengths of evidence.
questions.explanation: Operational metrics can reveal patterns worth watching, but causal conclusions require stronger designs. For further study, review descriptive analytics versus causal inference.

questions.prompt: A pack shows high scores and low replay rates. Which interpretation is most balanced?
choices: Learners may find it easy, complete it once, or not see value in replay; more evidence is needed
The pack is definitely perfect
The pack is definitely useless
Replay rate alone proves learning quality
questions.hint: Avoid overreading one pair of metrics.
questions.explanation: High scores and low replay can have multiple explanations, so analysts should combine metrics with learner feedback or item-level evidence. For further study, review triangulation and mixed-method evaluation.
